content.text: Template customization variables and hooks are the mechanisms through which agent starter templates accept team-specific configuration and execute custom logic at defined points in the scaffolding lifecycle. Variables parameterize static template content — project names, team identifiers, model providers, feature flags — so that a single template source can produce correctly configured projects for many teams and contexts. Hooks are executable scripts or functions that run at specific lifecycle stages during project generation, enabling teams to perform actions that cannot be expressed as simple string substitution.

Together, variables and hooks form the extension surface of a template: the set of inputs and callbacks that a template exposes so that teams can customize output without modifying the template source itself. A well-designed variable schema tells teams exactly what they must provide and what they may optionally override. A well-designed hook interface defines where custom logic may execute and what context it receives, making the behavior of project generation predictable and auditable even when team-supplied code runs.

Templates without customization mechanisms produce rigid, one-size-fits-all projects that teams immediately begin modifying by hand. Those manual modifications diverge from the template immediately after generation and cannot benefit from future template upgrades. Variables solve the personalization problem at generation time; hooks solve the automation problem for actions that fall outside pure file templating. Both mechanisms are essential for templates that remain in long-term use across a large organization.

Standardizing the variable schema and hook interface across all templates in a platform also yields operational benefits. Platform teams can validate inputs before generation, catching configuration errors before they propagate into committed code. CI systems can replay generation with different variable values to test template behavior. Security teams can audit hook scripts centrally without reviewing each team's custom code. These benefits compound as the number of teams using the platform grows.
Single template source serves many teams with different naming, ownership, and configuration requirements.
Variables document required configuration explicitly, reducing onboarding friction for new teams.
Hooks enable automation — secret rotation, registry registration, notification — at generation time.
Lifecycle boundaries make hook behavior predictable and auditable by platform teams.
Separation of template logic from team customization enables independent upgrade paths.
Input validation on variable schemas prevents misconfigured projects from reaching repositories.
Complex variable schemas increase the cognitive load of template configuration.
Hooks introduce executable code at generation time, expanding the attack surface if untrusted code runs.
Deeply nested conditional logic driven by feature flags makes template maintenance harder.
Teams may work around the hook interface by modifying generated files, defeating the template model.
Schema versioning must be managed carefully when template updates add or remove variables.

A template engine — Cookiecutter, Copier, Jinja2, or a custom tool — reads a schema file that declares each variable: its name, type, default value, description, and validation constraints. When a developer invokes the template, the tool prompts for required variables (or reads them from a configuration file), validates the inputs, and then renders each template file by substituting variable references with their resolved values. Conditional blocks in templates use boolean variables or enumeration values to include or exclude entire file sections or directories.

Hooks are scripts placed in reserved directories — typically `hooks/pre_gen_project/`, `hooks/post_gen_project/`, and similar lifecycle directories — that the template engine discovers and executes at the corresponding lifecycle stage. The engine passes a context object or environment variables to each hook, containing the resolved variable values so that hook logic can branch on configuration. Post-generation hooks commonly register the new project with internal registries, create initial Jira tickets, configure secrets in a vault, or send notifications to team channels.

The combination of variables and hooks means that the template engine controls the rendering pipeline while teams control two surfaces: the variable values they supply and the hook scripts they author or choose from a library of approved scripts. Platform teams can enforce constraints on both surfaces — required variables cannot be empty, hook scripts must pass a signature check before execution — without preventing legitimate customization.

attrs.alt: Template customization variables and hooks architecture

content.text: Introduce a formal variable schema and hook interface when the platform serves more than a handful of teams and template outputs already vary significantly between teams. Without a formal schema, team-specific customizations live in ad-hoc post-generation scripts or manual edits, both of which break the upgrade path. The investment in schema design and hook interface documentation pays off when teams begin requesting upgrades to templates they customized months earlier.

Limit hook execution to trusted scripts — scripts committed to the template repository or approved from a platform-managed library — especially in automated generation pipelines. Allowing arbitrary hook scripts from external sources creates a code execution vector that bypasses normal code review. In lower-trust contexts, constrain hooks to a declarative format (YAML-defined steps) rather than arbitrary scripts to maintain auditability without sacrificing flexibility.

# copier.yaml — variable schema definition
_subdirectory: template

service_name:
  type: str
  help: "Short lowercase name for the service (used in package names and identifiers)"
  validator: "{% if not service_name.isidentifier() %}Must be a valid Python identifier{% endif %}"

team_name:
  type: str
  help: "Owning team name (used in CODEOWNERS and runbook headers)"

model_provider:
  type: str
  choices:
    - anthropic
    - openai
    - bedrock
  default: anthropic
  help: "Primary LLM provider for generated agent client code"

addons:
  type: str
  multiselect: true
  choices:
    - postgres
    - redis
    - s3
    - slack-notifier
  default: []
  help: "Optional infrastructure add-ons to scaffold"

feature_flags:
  type: dict
  default:
    enable_tracing: true
    enable_cost_alerts: true
    enable_eval_suite: false
  help: "Feature flags controlling optional generated components"

cost_center:
  type: str
  help: "Cost center code for tagging generated cloud resources"

# hooks/pre_gen_project.py — runs before file rendering
import re, sys

service_name = '{{ service_name }}'
team_name = '{{ team_name }}'

# Validate service name format
if not re.match(r'^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,30}$', service_name):
    print(f"ERROR: service_name '{service_name}' must be 2-30 lowercase chars, digits, or hyphens")
    sys.exit(1)

# Validate team exists in internal directory (pseudo-code)
# known_teams = load_team_directory()
# if team_name not in known_teams:
#     print(f"ERROR: team '{team_name}' not found in team directory")
#     sys.exit(1)

print(f"Pre-gen validation passed for {service_name} owned by {team_name}")

# hooks/post_gen_project.py — runs after file rendering
import os, subprocess, sys, json

context = json.loads(os.environ.get('COPIER_ANSWERS', '{}'))
service_name = context.get('service_name', '{{ service_name }}')
team_name = context.get('team_name', '{{ team_name }}')
model_provider = context.get('model_provider', 'anthropic')

# 1. Register service in internal catalog
result = subprocess.run(
    ['service-catalog', 'register',
     '--name', service_name,
     '--owner', team_name,
     '--template-version', '{{ _copier_conf.src_path }}'],
    capture_output=True, text=True
)
if result.returncode != 0:
    print(f"WARNING: Service catalog registration failed: {result.stderr}")
    # Non-fatal: team can register manually

# 2. Initialize eval suite if enabled
if '{{ feature_flags.enable_eval_suite }}' == 'True':
    subprocess.run(['eval-runner', 'init', '--service', service_name], check=False)

# 3. Print next steps
print(f"\nProject {service_name} generated successfully.")
print(f"Next steps:")
print(f"  cd {service_name}")
print(f"  git init && git add . && git commit -m 'chore: initial scaffold'")
print(f"  Create GitHub repo and push to origin")

A team generating a new Slack-integrated alert agent sets `service_name=alert-router`, `team_name=platform-sre`, `model_provider=anthropic`, `addons=[slack-notifier]`, and `feature_flags.enable_cost_alerts=true`. The template engine renders the Anthropic client configuration, the Slack notification module from the `slack-notifier` add-on, and the cost alerting component. The pre-gen hook validates the service name format and team directory lookup. The post-gen hook registers the service in the internal catalog and prints setup instructions.

A second team generating a data-processing agent sets `addons=[postgres, s3]` and `feature_flags.enable_eval_suite=true`. The postgres add-on includes a database connection pool module and migration scripts. The s3 add-on includes boto3 client configuration. The eval suite flag enables generation of the `evals/` directory with baseline test fixtures. The post-gen hook runs `eval-runner init` to create the eval suite configuration in the platform eval registry.

In an automated pipeline, a CI job runs template generation with a fixed set of variables drawn from a team configuration file stored in the internal developer portal. Variable values are read from a YAML file, validated against the schema without interactive prompts, and the rendered project is committed to a new branch for team review. This pattern ensures that generated projects are always reproducible from the variable configuration file, enabling teams to regenerate or upgrade by updating the configuration file and re-running the pipeline.

Stripe's internal developer platform uses a scaffolding tool (described in engineering blog posts on developer experience) that accepts team metadata as variables and executes post-generation hooks to register services in their service ownership database, create PagerDuty schedules, and provision initial Vault paths for secrets. The hooks are written as declarative YAML steps rather than arbitrary scripts, each step invoking an approved platform CLI command. This design allows Stripe to audit every action a hook takes, replay generation in a dry-run mode, and upgrade hook behavior centrally without requiring teams to update their generated projects.
Making all variables required, forcing teams to look up values they don't know at generation time. Provide sensible defaults for optional configuration.
Allowing arbitrary shell scripts as hooks without signature verification, creating a code execution attack vector.
Using too many feature flags, producing a template with exponential conditional complexity that is difficult to test and maintain.
Embedding secrets or credentials in variable values; hook scripts should fetch secrets from a vault at generation time using team identity, not accept them as inputs.
Failing to version the variable schema, making it impossible to regenerate or upgrade projects scaffolded from older template versions.
Running hooks with elevated permissions not required by any hook action; hooks should operate with the minimum privilege needed for their specific actions.
Define a JSON Schema or equivalent machine-readable schema for all variables so that validation runs before any file rendering begins.
Provide a `answers.yaml` or equivalent file for non-interactive generation; commit this file with the generated project so the generation is reproducible.
Test pre-gen and post-gen hooks in isolation using unit tests before embedding them in the template; hooks that fail silently are harder to debug than hooks with explicit error messages.
Keep hooks idempotent: running a hook twice for the same project should not create duplicate catalog entries, duplicate PagerDuty schedules, or duplicate Vault paths.
Document each variable's purpose, valid values, and effect on generated output in the schema file itself; teams should not need to read template source to understand what a variable does.
Use a dry-run mode for hooks in CI pipelines: hooks in dry-run mode print what they would do without making external API calls, enabling template CI to verify hook logic without side effects.

Variable schema validation and file rendering are fast operations even for templates with dozens of variables and hundreds of files; generation time is dominated by hook execution latency. Hooks that make synchronous API calls — to service catalogs, vault APIs, or ticketing systems — can take 5–30 seconds each, which is acceptable in interactive use but significant in automated pipelines that generate many projects in parallel. Hooks that can operate asynchronously or that can be deferred to background jobs should be structured accordingly.

At scale, maintaining variable schema compatibility across many teams who have committed `answers.yaml` files from older template versions is the primary operational burden. Each time a variable is renamed, removed, or its type changes, teams attempting to upgrade must update their configuration files. Mitigate this burden by deprecating variables over multiple template versions rather than removing them immediately, and by providing a migration script that updates `answers.yaml` files from one schema version to the next.
Medium — platform must maintain step library
Medium — hook scripts need their own tests
Platform teams with mature review processes
Very high — any code may run
High — plugin compatibility must be maintained

Hook execution is the highest-risk operation in the template lifecycle because it runs code at generation time, often on developer workstations with broad local permissions. Enforce that all hooks are committed to the template repository and pass code review before they can execute. For hooks that make external API calls, use short-lived tokens issued to the developer's identity rather than long-lived service account credentials embedded in the template. Audit logs of hook executions — which hooks ran, what variables were in context, what external calls were made — provide the evidence needed to investigate incidents caused by hook failures or misuse.

Variable schema validation must reject inputs that could cause injection vulnerabilities in hook scripts. Service names, team names, and other string variables that are interpolated into shell commands within hooks must be validated against strict patterns before they reach hook execution. Use parameterized command invocation (subprocess with argument arrays) in hooks rather than string interpolation into shell commands, so that even if variable validation is bypassed, injected content cannot alter command structure.
Variables parameterize template content at generation time; hooks execute custom logic at defined lifecycle stages.
A formal variable schema with validation prevents misconfigured projects from reaching repositories.
Hooks should be trusted code (committed, reviewed) with minimum privilege and idempotent behavior.
Declarative hook steps offer higher auditability than arbitrary scripts in regulated or large-scale environments.
Commit a reproducible answers file with each generated project to enable future upgrades without re-entering configuration.
Schema versioning and migration tooling are necessary operational investments as templates evolve.
